title: Model Distillation & Engineering Performance Metrics

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: Our smaller model needs to behave like the large one. We should try knowledge distillation.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: Yes, distillation lets the student model learn the soft probability outputs of the teacher.

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: This will save us millions of parameters, allowing faster edge inference.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: How are we coordinating the write across the user service and the billing service?

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: It's a distributed transaction. We'll need a coordination protocol like two-phase commit.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: Is two-phase commit fast enough, or does it introduce too much latency?

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: It is slow, but we need strict consistency for financial transactions.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: On our go-to-market strategy: we are relying on product-led growth.

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: Yes, our self-serve trial will let users experience value before upgrading.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: A clean self-serve flow will reduce our sales cycles significantly.

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: When we review the sprint performance, let's keep defensiveness out of the room.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: Agreed. Defensiveness blocks learning. We should encourage active feedback.

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: Yes, seeking active feedback regularly helps us catch process gaps early.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: For our investments, we need hedging to lower portfolio drawdowns.

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: Agreed. Hedging across sectors helps protect our capital.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: But how are we measuring team productivity? Lines of code?

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: No, lines of code is a terrible proxy for productivity. It leads to metric manipulation.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: Right, Goodhart's Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: Exactly. If we target line counts, developers will just write bloated code.

conversation.speaker: Rahul
conversation.text: Let's focus on feature delivery velocity and system reliability metrics instead.

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: Agreed. I'll write the design doc for the distributed transaction setup.

vocabulary.meaning: the process of transferring knowledge from a large model (teacher) to a smaller one (student)
a numerical value in a model that is learned during the training process
a database transaction that updates data across two or more networked systems
a protocol (like 2-Phase Commit) that ensures distributed nodes commit or abort together
a business strategy where user acquisition and expansion are driven by the product itself
allowing users to onboard and pay for a product without interacting with sales
the tendency to react with sensitivity or anger when receiving constructive feedback
proactively seeking evaluative inputs to improve performance or processes
an investment position intended to offset potential losses or gains that may be incurred by a companion investment
the peak-to-trough decline during a specific record period of an investment or portfolio
an indirect metric or value used to represent a target that is difficult to measure directly
gaming a measurement system to meet targets without improving the underlying quality (Goodhart's Law)

takeaway: Relying on proxy metrics often leads to systemic gaming; systems and teams must be evaluated on real, direct outcomes.
